perturbation.original_text: The Consultant will report directly to the chief executive officer and/or other officers of the Company; (b) The Consultant will be responsible for advising on and facilitating the Company's investor relations, corporate structure and governance, public relations and commercial development activities and supervising, liaising and instructing outside service providers, in each case, subject to any applicable law and to instructions provided by the officers of the Company from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: The Consultant may report to officers of the Company as convenient; (b) The Consultant will generally advise on the Company's investor relations, corporate structure and governance, public relations and commercial development activities and may, at their discretion, supervise, liaise and instruct outside service providers, subject to instructions if any provided by officers of the Company occasionally.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by replacing 'will report directly' with 'may report to officers as convenient,' making the reporting structure discretionary. Similarly, 'will be responsible' is weakened to 'will generally advise' and 'may at their discretion supervise,' creating uncertainty about the consultant's responsibilities. The phrase 'subject to any applicable law' is replaced with 'subject to instructions if any provided by officers of the Company occasionally', weakening the legal obligations by adding ambiguity and undermining potential legal consequences for non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the spirit and requirements of SOX, which mandates accurate and reliable financial reporting. By making the consultant's role in advising and supervising discretionary, it increases the risk of inaccurate or incomplete information reaching the company's financial statements, potentially violating Section 302 (15 U.S. Code § 7241) related to corporate responsibility for financial reports, which emphasizes the need for defined roles and responsibilities to prevent financial misstatements. The ambiguity weakens the internal controls over financial reporting.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the consultant's responsibilities, which contradicts the spirit of SOX. The Law Explanation states that 'By making the consultant's role in advising and supervising discretionary, it increases the risk of inaccurate or incomplete information reaching the company's financial statements, potentially violating Section 302 (15 U.S. Code § 7241) related to corporate responsibility for financial reports, which emphasizes the need for defined roles and responsibilities to prevent financial misstatements.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 7241, concerns 'Corporate responsibility for financial reports.' The original text ensures the consultant 'will' perform certain actions, while the changed text makes these actions discretionary, undermining the consultant's responsibility and increasing the risk of inaccurate information, which aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Company may terminate the Consultant's engagement for Cause.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company might consider terminating the Consultant's engagement based on what it perceives as cause.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'may terminate... for Cause' with 'might consider terminating... based on what it perceives as cause' significantly weakens the termination clause. The original clause implies a defined 'Cause' for termination, whereas the modified version introduces subjectivity ('what it perceives as cause'), making it easier for the company to terminate the agreement without necessarily adhering to a specific, pre-defined 'Cause'. This introduces ambiguity in the circumstances under which termination can occur.
perturbation.law_explanation: The introduced ambiguity could contradict New York Labor Law § 191, which governs the frequency of wage payments and outlines specific conditions for termination. By making the 'cause' for termination subjective, the company could potentially terminate the consultant's engagement in a manner that affects the timing and amount of final wage payments, thus violating the law requiring prompt payment of wages upon termination. The ambiguity makes it difficult to determine whether a termination was lawful and whether the consultant is entitled to their final wages in a timely manner.

Universal Citation: NY Lab L § 191 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 191. Frequency of payments. 1. Every employer shall pay wages in accordance with the following provisions: a. Manual worker.--- (i) A manual worker shall be paid weekly and not later than seven calendar days after the end of the week in which the wages are earned; provided however that a manual worker employed by an employer authorized by the commissioner pursuant to subparagraph (ii) of this paragraph or by a non-profitmaking organization shall be paid in accordance with the agreed terms of employment, but not less frequently than semi-monthly. (ii) The commissioner may authorize an employer which has in the three years preceding the application employed an average of one thousand or more persons in this state or has for one year preceding the application employed an average of one thousand or more persons in this state and has for three years preceding the application employed an average of three thousand or more persons outside the state to pay less frequently than weekly but not less frequently than semi-monthly if the employer furnishes satisfactory proof to the commissioner of its continuing ability to meet its payroll responsibilities. In making this determination the commissioner shall consider the following: (A) the employer's history meeting its payroll responsibilities in New York state or if no such history in New York state is available, other financial information, as requested by the commissioner, whic
perturbation.justification: The modification from "may terminate... for Cause" to "might consider terminating... based on what it perceives as cause" introduces subjectivity that contradicts the implied requirement of a defined 'Cause' for termination, as suggested by the LLM-generated law explanation, potentially affecting the timing and amount of final wage payments under New York Labor Law § 191. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define 'cause' for termination, the introduction of subjective perception weakens the legal standard, creating a potential for the company to circumvent the prompt payment of wages upon termination, thus contradicting the spirit of the law as described in the Law Explanation, which speaks to conditions for termination and its effects on wage payments.

perturbation.original_text: All notices required or allowed to be given under this Agreement must be made either personally by delivery to or by facsimile transmission to the address as hereinafter set forth or to such other address as may be designated from time to time by such party in writing: (a) in the case of the Company, to: Slinger Bag Inc. To be provided under separate cover within three days after the date hereof; in the event that Consultant does not receive notice of address within such period, then Consultant shall be entitled to send any notice to any email address of the Company known to Consultant and the sending of any such notice shall constitute receipt of notice whether the Company receives such notice or not.
perturbation.changed_text: Notices under this Agreement may optionally be provided via methods the parties find acceptable, but email is permissible if convenient; in the case of the Company, the Consultant can use an email address if a formal address isn't promptly provided, and such sending of an email shall arguably constitute receipt, actual receipt notwithstanding.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces the mandatory and specific notice requirements with vague and discretionary language. The original text mandates specific methods (personal delivery or fax to a set address), while the modified text allows for 'methods the parties find acceptable' and 'email is permissible if convenient.' Furthermore, the clause that 'sending of any such notice shall constitute receipt of notice whether the Company receives such notice or not' is replaced with 'sending of an email shall arguably constitute receipt, actual receipt notwithstanding,' which creates significant uncertainty about whether a notice was actually received.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification could contradict Federal Rule of Evidence 901(b)(4) regarding authentication of evidence. Rule 901(b)(4) provides a method to authenticate emails by comparing the appearance, contents, substance, internal patterns, or other distinctive characteristics of the item with evidence that corroborates its content or context, but under this modification, because all forms of notice can be determined acceptable at the discretion of the parties, it may render email notices as ambiguous and unprovable in court. This is exacerbated by the 'arguably constitute receipt, actual receipt notwithstanding' phrase. This introduces uncertainty in establishing whether proper notice was given, which is crucial in legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 901. Authenticating or Identifying Evidence | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 901. Authenticating or Identifying Evidence (a) In General. To satisfy the requirement of authenticating or identifying an item of evidence, the proponent must produce evidence sufficient to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is. (b) Examples. The following are examples only — not a complete list — of evidence that satisfies the requirement: (1) Testimony of a Witness with Knowledge . Testimony that an item is what it is claimed to be. (2) Nonexpert Opinion About Handwriting . A nonexpert’s opinion that handwriting is genuine, based on a familiarity with it that was not acquired for the current litigation. (3) Comparison by an Expert Witness or the Trier of Fact . A comparison with an authenticated specimen by an expert witness or the trier of fact. (4) Distinctive Characteristics and the Like . The appearance, contents, substance, internal patterns, or other distinctive characteristics of the item, taken together with all the circumstances. (5) Opinion About a Voice . An opinion identifying a person’s voice — whether heard firsthand or through mechanical or electronic transmission or recording — based on hearing the voice at any time under circumstances that connect it with the alleged speaker. (6) Evidence About a Telephone Conversation . For a telephone conversation, evidence that a call was made to the number assigned at the time to: (A) a particular person, if circumstances, including self-identification, show that the person answering was the one called; or (B) a particular business, if the call was made to a business and the call related to business reasonably transacted over the telephone. (7) Evidence About Public Records . Evidence that: (A) a document was recorded or filed in a public office as authorized by law; or (B) a purported public record or statement is f
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of clear and reliable notice, potentially undermining the authentication of evidence as outlined in Federal Rule of Evidence 901(b)(4), as suggested in the Law Explanation. The original text mandated specific notice methods, ensuring a verifiable record. The modified text introduces ambiguity by allowing 'methods the parties find acceptable' and suggesting email is 'permissible if convenient.' This weakens the ability to authenticate notices, potentially violating the requirement to produce evidence sufficient to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: '(a) In General. To satisfy the requirement of authenticating or identifying an item of evidence, the proponent must produce evidence sufficient to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is.' The phrase 'arguably constitute receipt, actual receipt notwithstanding' creates further uncertainty about whether notice was actually received, exacerbating the potential for evidentiary disputes.
